title: Salient Cues and Complexity
abstract: Important decisions are often complex, and existing evidence suggests that complexity can affect economic behavior. It is an open question, however, exactly when and how complexity matters. We hypothesize that salient cues—standing out in the choice context—mitigate the effect of complexity on choices. We theoretically develop and experimentally test this hypothesis in the context of portfolio selection. We find that, in both simple and complex problems, subjects seek highly right-skewed portfolios, which have an extreme and salient upside, and avoid highly left-skewed portfolios with an extreme and salient downside. Complexity does affect, however, choices among symmetric portfolios, which have neither a salient upside nor downside. Absent a salient cue, subjects diversify naively. Evidence on response times and memory supports our salience-based explanation.

author.Markus Dertwinkel-Kalt: University of Münster, 48149 Münster, Germany;Max Planck Institute for Research on Collective Goods, 53113 Bonn, Germany;

article.1. Introduction: Important decisions, such as choosing an insurance or retirement savings plan, are often complex. It is an open question, however, exactly when and how complexity affects economic behavior. Existing evidence suggests that complexity can result in people ignoring (part of the) relevant information, which, in turn, affects the choices they make (e.g.,Abeler and Jäger 2015,Bhargava et al. 2017,Breunig et al. 2021,Banovetz and Oprea 2023,Enke et al. 2023). At the same time, even in arguably simple and transparent choice problems, people tend to ignore relevant information: they often base their choices only on the most salient aspects of the available options (e.g.,Dertwinkel-Kalt and Köster 2020,Li and Camerer 2022). By directing attention to a subset of the relevant information, salient cues—standing out in the choice context—might, thus, mitigate the effect of complexity on choices. A better understanding of exactly when and how complexity affects economic behavior is a prerequisite for designing effective (information) policies that help consumers navigate the complex decisions they regularly face. In this paper, we study whether salient cues indeed mitigate the effect of complexity on choices. Extending salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012,2022), we formulate and experimentally test two hypotheses. Our first hypothesis states that, whether or not a problem is complex, people base their decisions on highly salient aspects of the available options. Put differently, salience effects trump the effect of complexity on choices. For complex problems without such a salient cue, however, we hypothesize that people revert to even simpler rules of thumb. And, as a consequence, we expect complexity to matter absent a salient cue. We provide experimental evidence in the context of choice under risk supporting both hypotheses. In doing so, we make progress on the broader question of when and how complexity affects economic behavior. An experimental design that allows us to test our hypotheses must have three features. First, we need to find an environment in which we can precisely define complexity and salience. Second, we have to develop a theory of how complexity and salience interact in affecting choices. Third, we have to construct problems that allow us to switch complexity on and off, holding the choice problem itself and salience fixed. Our design accommodates all three features. We start inSection 2by introducing an environment that meets all criteria: portfolio selection problems of how to allocate a given budget across two assets. In portfolio selection problems, a layer of computational complexity arises naturally. As illustrated inFigure 1, to understand the consequences of choosing one portfolio over another, subjects have to first calculate the outcomes of these portfolios. By doing the math for the subjects, we can easily manipulate the complexity of the problem, holding the problem itself fixed. Our definition of salience is based salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012)—the leading salience model in the literature. Unlike more superficial notions of salience that refer to framing, size, or color, this notion of salience is linked to the outcomes of the available options. More specifically, in what we refer to as the contrast effect, relatively large contrasts in outcomes are salient and, thus, attract attention.1As we formally show inSection 2, this implies that the salience of its outcomes is closely linked to the skewness of a risk. A right-skewed risk has an extreme and, therefore, salient upside, whereas a left-skewed risk has an extreme and salient downside. A symmetric risk, on the other hand, has neither a particularly salient upside nor downside. We can, thus, vary the existence of a salient cue—corresponding to a particularly salient outcome—by comparing portfolio selection problems with highly skewed assets to problems with symmetric assets. In sum, portfolio selection problems allow us to precisely define and easily vary both complexity and salience. Still, because we vary salience by changing the assets and thereby the actual choice problem, testing our hypotheses requires a theory of how people behave in the presence and absence of a salient cue. We focus on the choice between a binary portfolio and the safe option paying its expected value with certainty. FollowingBordalo et al. (2012), we assume that subjects are intrinsically risk-neutral and, thus, indifferent between the two options. Salience and/or complexity can tilt choices toward either option, however. Because of its highly salient upside, salience theory predicts subjects will choose a highly right-skewed portfolio over the safe option. Similarly, because of its highly salient downside, salience theory predicts subjects will choose the safe option over a highly left-skewed risk. Crucially, we assume that, even in complex problems, subjects base their choices on such highly salient outcomes. Our first hypothesis then is that, no matter the complexity, subjects choose highly right-skewed and avoid highly left-skewed portfolios. As we outline inSection 3, despite not having a particularly salient upside or downside, salience theory predicts an aversion toward symmetric risks.2In simple problems, we expect that even such subtle salience effects are enough for subjects to choose the safe option. At the same time, given the lack of salience, we expect complexity to matter. Arguably, in complex problems with symmetric assets, subjects have a much harder time figuring out in which asset to invest more. We, thus, assume that, absent a highly salient outcome, subjects respond to complexity by diversifying naively; that is, they invest equal shares in the underlying assets. Intuitively, when having no clue which asset is the better investment, subjects abstain from taking a stance and simply split their budget equally. This assumption is also consistent with the experimental literature on portfolio selection (e.g.,Ackert et al. 2015,Eyster and Weizsäcker 2016,Ungeheuer and Weber 2021). Our second hypothesis then is that subjects avoid symmetric portfolios in simple problems, but they diversify naively in complex ones irrespective of the monetary consequences. We present our experimental design inSection 3. Subjects repeatedly choose between a binary portfolio and the safe option paying its expected value with certainty. Within subject, we vary the complexity of the problem: each subject makes multiple decisions in simple and complex problems. Between subjects, we randomize whether the underlying assets are skewed or symmetric and, therefore, whether a salient cue exists. The problems with skewed assets—and, by construction, skewed portfolios—are set up in such a way that naive diversification always results in choosing the safe option. Salience theory, in contrast, predicts subjects will choose sufficiently right-skewed risks over the safe option. If subjects indeed choose sufficiently right-skewed risks in simple and complex problems, we can conclude that they do not diversify naively in the presence of a salient cue. Instead, this behavior would be consistent with subjects using the cue to reach a decision. The problems with symmetric assets are set up in such a way that naive diversification sometimes results in risk-seeking behavior. Salience theory, in contrast, predicts subjects will always choose the safe option. Hence, if subjects indeed diversify naively in complex problems with symmetric assets, we can reject salience theory as an explanation. Going further, if subjects do avoid symmetric risks in simple problems, we can conclude that complexity affects choices absent a salient cue.3In sum, by allowing us to precisely distinguish the predictions of salience theory from naive diversification, our experimental design provides a clean test of our hypotheses on how complexity and salience interact in affecting choices. Section 4presents our main experimental results. We find that subjects choose highly right-skewed and avoid highly left-skewed portfolios no matter the complexity. Specifically, in both simple and complex problems, around 70% of the subjects choose a highly right-skewed portfolio over the safe alternative. Similarly, a large majority of subjects chooses the safe option when the alternative is highly left-skewed (i.e., 84% in simple problems and 75% in complex ones). Complexity, therefore, does not affect choices among highly skewed portfolios. This changes in problems with symmetric assets: most subjects choose the safe option in simple problems, but they diversify naively in complex ones. Specifically, in simple problems, around 74% of the subjects choose the safe option. This share drops significantly—by 31 percentage points (p.p.)—in complex problems because subjects diversify naively. Complexity therefore does affect choices among symmetric portfolios. When interpreted through the lens of our model, this combination of facts supports our main hypothesis: complexity affects choices only in the absence of a salient cue. InSection 5, we discuss the robustness of our main experimental results, and we document two additional facts that substantiate our salience-based explanation. We start by verifying that our results are robust to adding other forms of complexity. In particular, by increasing the number of available portfolios (to up to 101), we add what we call comparative complexity. Besides doing the math, subjects now have to evaluate and compare a large number of options. As predicted by salience theory, we find that a large majority of subjects picks a highly right-skewed portfolio even in this case. We conclude that neither computational nor comparative complexity affects choices among highly skewed portfolios. When choosing from a large set of symmetric portfolios, subjects again diversify naively. In fact, because of comparative complexity, the share of subjects choosing the safe option drops to as little as 14%. These findings lend further support to the idea that complexity affects choices only in the absence of a salient cue. Next, we try to shed more light on the mechanism underlying the observed interaction of skewness and complexity. One might be concerned, for example, that this interaction is simply the result of skewness preferences being rather strong (in a utility sense). In fact, if complexity makes decisions noisier and if the preference for right-skewed risks is stronger than the aversion toward symmetric ones, a classical random utility model could explain the observed interaction of skewness and complexity. Applying the methodology developed inAlós-Ferrer et al. (2021), we can use data on response times to rule out this alternative explanation. The idea is simple. Consistent with evidence from the psychology literature, a larger difference in (decision) utility should translate into fewer random errors and/or faster decisions. Hence, for strong skewness preferences to explain our results, at least one of the following two conditions has to hold in simple problems: either more subjects choose the right-skewed risks than avoid the symmetric ones (i.e., we observe fewer errors for skewed risks) or subjects are at least not systematically slower in choosing right-skewed risks than in avoiding symmetric ones. We find that neither condition holds in simple problems, suggesting that strong skewness preferences alone cannot explain our main experimental results. This also means that skewness and complexity do more than just shifting (decision) utility and introducing noise; something more fundamental has to change in how subjects reach a decision in complex problems with and without a salient cue. In a second step, we use memory data to provide more direct evidence for our salience-based explanation of the experimental results. We find that subjects are significantly better in recalling the (more salient) outcomes of highly skewed assets. This observation supports a notion of salience such as ours, which is closely linked to the skewness of a risk. Even when controlling for the time that a subject spent on the portfolio selection problem the subject has to recall, the skewness of the assets is predictive of memory. This directly speaks to the idea that the extreme outcomes of skewed assets are salient and, thus, come to mind more easily. And it is consistent with our assumption that, absent a salient cue, subjects apply a simple rule such as naive diversification, which is oblivious to the outcomes of the assets. In sum, the evidence suggests that complexity can fundamentally change how subjects approach a problem and reach a decision. We discuss the related literature inSection 6. Whereas there is a lot of work on how complexity and salience affect economic decisions, no paper has systematically studied the interaction of the two. We provide evidence on salience mitigating the effect of complexity on choices. On top, we make three contributions to the (experimental) literature on portfolio selection. First, the experimental literature almost exclusively studies portfolio selection problems with symmetric assets (e.g.,Ackert et al. 2015,Eyster and Weizsäcker 2016,Ungeheuer and Weber 2021), whereas we also consider problems with skewed assets. And our experimental results suggest that the predominant finding of subjects diversifying naively might be an artifact of exactly this focus on symmetric assets. Second, we qualify existing evidence on the relationship between financial literacy and underdiversification (seeHastings et al. 2013,Lusardi and Mitchell 2014for surveys). Whereas more literate subjects in our experiment are less likely to diversify naively, they are equally prone to choosing highly right-skewed portfolios over the safe alternative. Third, we provide evidence that subjects seek variance in highly right-skewed portfolios, thereby conclusively rejecting models on a variance–skewness trade-off (Mitton and Vorkink 2007). We conclude inSection 7by discussing some tentative policy implications of our findings. Economists traditionally equate rationality with consistency (Halevy and Mayraz 2024) and, therefore, often interpret consistent behavior as an indication of strong preferences. Our experimental results suggest instead that consistent behavior is not (necessarily) the result of strong preferences, but seems to be driven by people simply following salient cues. Whether salient cues improve complex decisions likely depends on the context, however. It might well be that, absent a salient cue, people are completely overwhelmed by a complex problem and make bad decisions in response. But it might also be the case that the cue itself makes people focus on the wrong aspects of the problem at hand, thereby distorting the decisions they make (see, e.g., section 6 inBordalo et al. 2022for a discussion). This highlights the need for future research to investigate the welfare implications of changing behavior by providing salient cues.
article.2. Salience and Complexity in Choice Und: Building on salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012), we develop a model of how salience and complexity interact in shaping choice under risk. We introduce the model and derive its behavioral implications in the context of portfolio selection problems. The general idea of the model is much broader than this specific context, however. The focus on portfolio selection problems allows us to substantially simplify the notation and achieve a tight connection between the model and our experimental design (which we describe inSection 3). We start by introducing the setup. 2.1. Portfolio Selection Problems and (Computational) Complexity Consider two lotteriesX1andX2(henceforth: assets). A portfolioX(α)≔αX1+(1−α)X2,α∈[0,1], is a convex combination of the two assets. We denote asA⊆[0,1]the set of admissible investments in assetX1, and we define the corresponding choice set asC(A)≔{X(α):α∈A}. A portfolio selection problem is then given by a tuple(A,C)=(A,C(A)). We say that two portfolio selection problems(A,C)and(A′,C′)are essentially equivalent if and only ifC=C′. 2.1.1. Complexity. Crucially, as long asA≠{0,1}, a form of computational complexity arises in portfolio selection problems. As illustrated inFigure 1, to understand the consequences of choosing one portfolio over another, a subject has to first calculate the outcomes of these portfolios. The problems presented in the left and right panels ofFigure 1, respectively, are essentially equivalent; that is, the choice setCis the same across problems. Still, the problem in the left panel is clearly more complex than that in the right panel. Whereas, in the right panel, the portfolio outcomes are readily observable, in the left panel, subjects have to first compute these outcomes; that is,Adiffers across the two problems. We say that a portfolio selection problem(A,C)is (computationally) complex if and only ifA≠{0,1}. Otherwise, the problem is said to be simple. 2.1.2. Additional Structure and Notation. We impose four restrictions on the set of portfolio selection problems that we consider in our main analysis. First, we assume that both assets are nonnegative, binary lotteries with the same expected value. As a consequence, all resulting portfolios have the same expected value, and a risk-neutral expected utility maximizer, therefore, is indifferent between all portfolios. Second, we consider only portfolio selection problems with a safe option that pays this expected value with certainty. A risk-averse expected utility maximizer always chooses this safe option. Third, we further assume that the two assets are perfectly correlated, which implies that all the resulting portfolios have at most two outcomes. Fourth, we assume that the choice set includes only two portfolios, and in what follows, we denote these two portfolios asX(α)≕AandX(β)≕Bfor someα,β∈[0,1]. Binary assets (and portfolios) simplify the exposition of our results. First, any binary lottery is uniquely characterized via its expected value, variance, and skewness (Ebert 2015): for anyE∈R,V∈R>0, andS∈R, there exists exactly one binary lotteryL=L(E,V,S)with expected valueE, varianceV, and skewnessS.4The parameters of lotteryL(E,V,S)are given by 2.2. A Model of Salience and Complexity We present a model of how salience and complexity interact in shaping portfolio choice. The core idea is simple. Selecting a portfolio can be a demanding task—the more so the more complex the problem is. But a salient cue, guiding attention and behavior, may help subjects in reaching a decision.5In complex problems without such a cue, subjects may rely on even simpler rules of thumb, sometimes making choices independently of the monetary consequences. 2.2.1. Salience. We start by introducing a measure of salience going back toBordalo et al. (2012). Research from psychology and economics suggests that salience is first and foremost shaped by “contrast with surroundings, surprise, and prominence” (seeBordalo et al. 2022, p. 524 and the references therein). In the context of portfolio selection problems, this translates into an outcome of assetX1being particularly salient if and only if it differs by a lot from the corresponding outcome of assetX2.Bordalo et al. (2012)formalize this idea via a so-called salience function. Formally, a state of the world refers to a tuple of outcomes(x1,x2)∈R≥0, one for each of the assets. A salience functionσ:R≥02→R≥0measures how salient a given state of the world is. A state (x1,x2) is the more salient the larger its salience valueσ(x1,x2). A salience function is symmetric, bounded, and continuous, and it satisfies two psychologically founded properties. By the ordering property, for anyϵ,ϵ′≥0withϵ+ϵ′>0, we haveσ(x+ϵ,y−ϵ′)>σ(x,y). Ordering, therefore, implies that a state of the world is the more salient the more the outcomes in this state differ; it captures contrast effects (e.g.,Schkade and Kahneman 1998,Kunar et al. 2017,Wolfe and Horowitz 2017). By diminishing sensitivity, for anyx≠yandϵ>0, we haveσ(x+ϵ,y+ϵ)<σ(x,y). This property reflects Weber’s law of perception and can be understood as a level effect: a given contrast in outcomes is more salient at a lower outcome level. The implications of contrast and level effects are not aligned in general. When necessary, we pin down the resulting trade-off by relying on the following class of salience functions: The parameterθcaptures the relative importance of contrast and level effects. The largerθis, the more important the contrast effect relative to the level effect. And asθbecomes large so that(θ+1)2(x1+x2+θ)2is close to one, the salience of a state is fully governed by the contrast in outcomes. 2.2.2. Salient Cues. We want to capture the intuitive idea that salience—by directing attention to a subset of the relevant states—helps with reaching a decision. This requires, however, that there is one particularly salient state of the world, which immediately draws a subject’s attention. Informally, we say that a salient cue exists if and only if there is a state of the world(x1,x2)∈R≥02with a salience valueσ(x1,x2)much larger than those of the other states. To develop an intuition for when a salient cue exists, consider the salience function in (2) and assume that the contrast effect is sufficiently strong relative to the level effect (i.e.,θis large). We then have This suggests that the distribution of salience values,σ(X1,X2), has a similar shape as the distribution of contrasts in outcomes,X1−X2. Recall that the assets have the same expected value. It follows that one of the states is particularly salient compared with the remaining ones if and only if the distribution of contrasts is sufficiently asymmetric (around its mean of zero). Crucially, even if the level effect becomes relatively more important, it is still the asymmetry of contrasts across states that drives a wedge between the salience of the different states of the world. As a consequence, the existence of a salient cue will be closely tied to the asymmetry of contrasts in outcomes. Following earlier work (Dertwinkel-Kalt and Köster 2020), we measure the asymmetry of contrasts in outcomes across the different states via the relative skewness of assetsX1andX2: Based on this, we say that a salient cue exists in the portfolio selection problem(A,C)if and only if the relative skewness of the underlying assets exceeds some thresholdκ(A,C), which reflects the objective complexity of the portfolio selection problem. Formally, This condition ensures that there exists a (subset of) particularly salient state(s) of the world. We allow the thresholdκ=κ(A,C)to vary with features of the portfolio selection problem.6We setκ= 0 for simple problems, and we assume that it increases as the problem becomes more complex. Intuitively, the more complex the problem is, the more distinctive the outcomes of the assets have to be to immediately catch a subject’s attention and guide the subject’s behavior. Whereas we lack a theory of what makes a problem objectively complex, it seems uncontroversial to assume thatκis smaller in what we call simple problems than in what we call complex problems. 2.2.3. Portfolio Selection. To close the model, we have to specify how subjects select a portfolio in the presence and absence of a salient cue. We subsequently specify behavior in either case. 2.2.3.1. Behavior in the Presence of a Salient Cue. We assume that, if a salient cue exists (i.e., (3) holds), subjects evaluate the available portfolios in light of this cue. This is similar in spirit to the idea of reason-based choice (Shafir et al. 1993): the cue provides the subject with a reason to deliberately invest more in one of the two assets. We capture the idea of deliberately following a salient cue by modeling the choice among portfolios salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012). A salient thinker evaluates portfolioArelative to portfolioB.7Denote asFthe joint distribution (over outcomes) of portfoliosAandB. The agent choosesAoverBif and only if 2.2.3.2. Behavior in the Absence of a Salient Cue. We assume that, absent a salient cue (i.e., (3) does not hold), the problem of selecting a portfolio becomes overwhelmingly complex so that subjects revert to an even simpler rule of thumb to reach a decision. FollowingBenartzi and Thaler (2001)and others, we assume that subjects diversify naively: they simply invest equal shares in the two assets. This captures the intuition that a subject who has no clue which asset is the better investment abstains from taking a stance and simply invests equal shares in both.
article.3. Experimental Design: We develop an experimental design that allows us to assess the empirical validity of our model. We assume that subjects base their decisions on salient cues (in line with salience theory), whereas, absent such a cue, they diversify naively. We have to construct comparable portfolio selection problems with and without a salient cue, which allow us to distinguish the predictions of salience theory from naive diversification. Because our notion of salience is based on contrast in outcomes, however, we have to vary the underlying assets to create a salient cue. This gives rise to a key design challenge: we have to construct portfolio selection problems with different underlying assets (and different resulting portfolios) that are as comparable as possible and also create meaningful variation that allows us to distinguish salience theory from naive diversification. We start by deriving the qualitative predictions of salience theory. 3.1. Qualitative Predictions of Salience Theory Consider the choice between a binary lottery and the safe option paying its expected value with certainty. A salient thinker chooses the binary lotteryL(E,V,S)over the safe optionEif and only if the upside of this lottery is more salient than its downside.8As illustrated inFigure 3, for any given expected value and variance, the upside of a binary lottery is salient if and only if its skewness exceeds a certain threshold. This follows from the fact that the contrast in the lottery’s upside and its expected value monotonically increases in its skewness, whereas the contrast on its downside monotonically decreases in its skewness. Hence, by the contrast effect, there exists some threshold valueS^=S^(E,V)such that the binary lotteryL(E,V,S)is chosen over the safe optionEif and only ifS>S^(Dertwinkel-Kalt and Köster 2020, proposition 3). Moreover, for any nonnegative binary lottery, the level effect implies thatS^>0; that is, all symmetric and, as a consequence, also all left-skewed lotteries are unattractive to a salient thinker. In sum, salience theory makes two qualitative predictions. First, by the level effect, it predicts an aversion toward symmetric risks. Second, by the contrast effect, it predicts skewness preferences: that is, a preference for highly right-skewed and an aversion toward highly left-skewed risks. We build our experimental design around these two qualitative predictions of salience theory.9 3.2. Portfolio Selection Problems with and Without a Salient Cue Our notion of salience is closely tied to the (relative) skewness of the risks that subjects face. First, the relative skewness of the two assets determines whether a salient cue exists. Second, the behavior of a salient thinker depends on the skewness of the available portfolios. We build our experimental design around a class of binary lotteries—so-called Mao pairs—that is ideally suited to study the effect of (relative) skewness on choices.10 (Mao Pair). LetE,V,S∈R>0. The binary lotteriesL(E,V,S)andL(E,V,−S)with a correlation coefficientρ=ρ(L(E,V,S),L(E,V,−S))denote aMao pair. The two lotteries of a Mao pair have the same expected value and variance but skewness opposite in sign. Also, the portfolios derived from Mao pairs have useful statistical properties. Consider a portfolio selection problem with assetsX1=L(E,V,S)andX2=L(E,V,−S)and assume that X1and X2are perfectly negatively correlated. Then, the following statements hold: X(α)=L(E,(2α−1)2V,sgn(2α−1)S). Var(X(α))=0if and only ifα=12. X1−X2=L(0,4V,S)and X(α)−X(1/2)=L(0,(2α−1)2V,sgn(2α−1)S). By part I, any portfolio derived from perfectly negatively correlated Mao lotteries is again a binary lottery. Moreover, by part II, naive diversification results in choosing the safe option that pays the assets’ expected value with certainty. Hence, by letting subjects choose from the set We expect subjects to behave in line with salience theory whenever a salient cue exists, which depends on the relative skewness of the assets. By part III, the relative skewness of Mao lotteries takes a particularly simple form: the relative skewness of assetsX1andX2is given byS> 0. This implies that we can create a salient cue by simply choosing a sufficiently skewed Mao pair. To be precise, fix some complex portfolio selection problem withAc={α,1/2}and a choice setCc={X(α),X(1/2)}. Then, byEquation (3), a salient cue exists if and only ifS≥κc=κ(Ac,Cc). Combining this with the considerations in the preceding paragraph, our model makes a clear-cut prediction: subjects chooseX(α)overX(1/2)if and only ifα>1/2andS>max{S^,κc}. Going further, part III tells us that we can switch the complexity of the problem on and off without changing the relative skewness of the assets. Specifically, the relative skewness ofX(α)andX(1/2)is also exactlyS. Hence, if there exists a salient cue in the complex portfolio selection problem(Ac,Cc), the same is true for its essentially equivalent, but simple counterpart. (Salient Cue).Consider a perfectly negatively correlated Mao pair with S large enough. Subjects choose the same portfolio in the complex problem(Ac,Cc)and its essentially equivalent, but simple counterpart. Subjects deviate from naive diversification if and only ifα>12. Our model predicts that, if the underlying assets (and the resulting portfolios) are sufficiently skewed, behavior is the same across simple and complex problems. Absent a salient cue, we expect complexity to matter, however. To test for an interaction of salience and complexity, we must construct portfolio selection problems without a cue that are otherwise comparable. As a natural first step, we take the limit of a Mao pair asS→0. This yields a comparable portfolio selection problem in which the relative skewness of the assets is zero. Hence, unless the problem is simple, we predict subjects to diversify naively, which means that they will choose the safe option that pays the assets’ expected value with certainty (Lemma 1, part II). This is problematic, however, in that salience theory makes the same prediction: namely, it predicts an aversion toward symmetric risks. Hence, this class of portfolio selection problems does not allow us to distinguish between the predictions of salience theory and naive diversification. We address this problem by extending the experimental design proposed inEyster and Weizsäcker (2016). We construct a class of essentially equivalent portfolio selection problems in which the relative skewness of the assets is still zero but salience theory predicts behavior different from naive diversification. The following definition uncovers the mathematical structure of the so-called twin problems thatEyster and Weizsäcker (2016)use in their experiment.11 (Twin Problem). Consider the symmetric and perfectly negatively correlated lotteriesX1=L(E,V,0)andX2=L(E,γ2V,0)forγ∈[0,3]and defineX(α,γ)≔αX1+(1−α)X2. For someγ′≠1, atwin problemconsists of the following two portfolio selection problems: (P.1)C={X(α,1):α∈A}andA={3−γ′4,12}. (P.2)C={X(α,γ′):α∈A}andA={12,γ′1+γ′}. As described, we obtain (P.1) of a twin problem by taking the limit (forS→0) of a portfolio selection problem that is based on a Mao pair. To distinguish naive diversification from an aversion toward symmetric risks, we construct the essentially equivalent problem (P.2). Crucially, whereas naive diversification yields the safe option in (P.1), it does not in (P.2). This is illustrated inFigure 4. Portfolio 1 in the left panel implements the binary lottery that yields with equal probability either 96 or 72, which is exactly the same distribution over outcomes that portfolio 2 in the right panel implements. Portfolio 2 in the left panel as well as portfolio 1 in the right panel yield the assets’ expected value of 84 with certainty. Hence, by diversifying naively, subjects choose the portfolio with less variance in outcomes in the left panel but the portfolio with more variance in outcomes in the right panel. This can be summarized as follows. For any twin problem, the following statements hold: (P.1) and (P.2) are essentially equivalent portfolio selection problems. Naive diversification yields the safe option in (P.1) but not in (P.2). As before, we are interested in how behavior varies across complex and essentially equivalent but simple problems. We predict subjects will choose the safe option in simple problems. Because the relative skewness of the underlying assets is zero, however, we also expect subjects to diversify naively in complex problems. This suggests that complexity affects the portfolios that subjects choose (in (P.2) of the twin problems). We obtain a second testable prediction. (No Cue). Subjects do not always choose the same portfolio in a twin problem and its essentially equivalent but simple counterpart. Subjects diversify naively in the complex problems but choose the safe option in the simple version. 3.3. Implementation of the Experiment We implement a between-subjects design with multiple decisions per subject and two randomly assigned treatments. In the treatmentSkewed, subjects have to choose between different portfolios that are derived from Mao lotteries.Table 1provides an overview of the Mao pairs we use. In the treatmentSymmetric, on the other hand, subjects have to choose between portfolios that form a twin problem.Table 2provides an overview of the twin problems we implement. To ensure incentive compatibility, all assets that we use are nonnegative binary lotteries.12 Table 1.Mao Pairs Used in the TreatmentSkewed Table 1.Mao Pairs Used in the TreatmentSkewed Notes. The expected value of the assets is denoted byE, their variance is denoted byVand their skewness is denoted byS. On top of the main part of our design (as described inSection 3.2), we implement two additional sets of portfolio selection problems. First, in both treatments, we also implement portfolio selection problems with larger choice sets (seeAppendix Afor the extended definition of twin problems). This allows us to test the robustness of our main results to other forms of complexity. We derive the predictions of salience theory inAppendix B, and we discuss the corresponding experimental results inSection 5. Second, inSkewed, we implement all Mao pairs inTable 1not only under the perfectly negative correlation, but also under the maximal positive correlation. This provides us with additional variation in relative skewness. We derive the corresponding salience predictions inAppendix B, and we provide the experimental results inAppendix E. Each subject goes through three blocks of choices: (1) subjects make 12 choices between exactly two portfolios; (2) subjects make 12 choices from a larger set of portfolios, including those in the first block; and (3) subjects make the exact same choices as in the first block with the only exception that the outcomes of the two portfolios are made readily observable to the subjects.13When computing the final outcomes of the portfolios in Block 3, we round all numbers to the next integer. In Block 1 ofSkewed, we randomize the shareα∈{0.10,0.25,0.75,0.90}that the risky portfolio invests in the right-skewed asset at the subject–Mao pair level. This allows us to elicit preferences toward both right- and left-skewed risks. We further randomize in Block 1 (at the subject level) the order in which the two portfolios are displayed (see Figure D.6 for a screenshot of the decision screen). When making the outcomes of the portfolios readily observable in Block 3, we implement not only the sameα∈{0.10,0.25,0.75,0.90}as in Block 1, but we also use the same order of portfolios as in Block 1. Finally, we randomize the order of blocks as well as the order of tasks within each block at the subject level. At the beginning of Block 1 and Block 2, subjects have to answer control questions to check for their comprehension of the portfolio selection tasks. After the last decision of the last block (which could be Block 1, Block 2, or Block 3), subjects face a surprise memory task, in which they have to recall the joint distribution of the assets presented on the preceding screen (seeFigure D.9for a screenshot). Subsequently, subjects answer five financial literacy questions (as inLusardi and Mitchell 2011,van Rooij et al. 2011) and five questions of a modified cognitive reflection test (CRT; closely aligned toPrimi et al. 2016), and they solve five math problems similar to computing the outcomes of a portfolio. All details on the additional questions can be found inAppendix D. Finally, we ask for demographics (including age, gender, field of study). During the experiment, outcomes are presented in units of an experimental currency (ECU). At the end of the experiment, for each subject, one of the 36 decisions inSkewedor 30 decisions inSymmetricis randomly drawn by the computer to be payoff-relevant. Also, the outcome of the payoff-relevant choice is randomly determined by the computer. Subjects receive feedback on their earnings only after the memory task and the financial literacy, modified CRT, and math questions. In addition, subjects are rewarded for their performance in the memory task (1 ECU per correctly remembered outcome) as well as for correct answers to the control, financial literacy, modified CRT, and math questions (1 ECU per correctly answered question). Experimental earnings are converted at a ratio of 4 ECU: €1. All subjects further receive a show-up fee of €4 for their willingness to participate in the experiment. We conducted 10 sessions withn= 296 subjects (146 subjects inSkewedand 150 subjects inSymmetric). The sessions took place in January 2020 at the experimental laboratory of the University of Cologne. The experiment lasted for around one hour, and average earnings were slightly higher than €25. Subjects were invited to the laboratory via ORSEE (Greiner 2015), and the experiment was implemented with the software z-Tree (Fischbacher 2007). The design of the experiment and its implementation were preregistered in the American Economic Association RCT registry.14
article.4. Main Experimental Result: As our main experimental result, we document that the effect of complexity on portfolio selection varies with the existence of a salient cue in a way that is consistent with our model. Main Result:Consistent withPrediction1,when the assets (as well as the risky portfolio) are sufficiently skewed, subjects choose the same portfolio in simple and complex problems. When the assets (as well as the risky portfolio) are symmetric, complexity changes behavior in a way consistent withPrediction2. We discuss the two parts separately.Figure 5summarizes the observation that complexity does not affect behavior in the presence of a salient cue.Figure 6illustrates the effect of complexity on behavior in the absence of such a cue. Additional results can be found inAppendix E. 4.1. Portfolio Selection Problems with a Cue If the problem is simple, subjects reveal an aversion toward left-skewed and a preference for sufficiently right-skewed risks. Such skewness preferences are consistent with salience theory (and also with our extension of the model). As predicted by our model and as summarized inFigure 5, we observe similar behavioral patterns in the essentially equivalent but complex portfolio selection problems. The results are robust to splitting the sample in different ways.15First, by comparing problems withA={0.25,0.5}to those withA={0.5,0.75}and problems withA={0.1,0.5}to those withA={0.5,0.9}, we can control for the variance in portfolio returns and identify the effect of skewness on choices.16Revealed skewness preferences do not depend on the variance of returns (seeFigures E.1andE.2). Second, subjects with above- and below-median cognitive skills as measured by the number of correct answers to the financial literacy, modified CRT, and math questions reveal similar skewness preferences (seeFigures E.3andE.4). 4.2. Portfolio Selection Problems Without a Cue In simple problems, a large majority of subjects chooses the safe option, thereby revealing an aversion toward symmetric risks. This share drops, however, from around 74% in simple problems to slightly more than 42% in (P.2) of complex twin problems. This is inconsistent with salience theory. But it is consistent with our model, according to which subjects diversify naively absent a salient cue. In sum, the revealed aversion toward symmetric risks in simple problems is not robust to increasing the problem’s complexity. More specifically, whereas, in (P.2) of a twin problem, subjects behave, on average, in a risk-seeking manner, they behave in a risk-averse manner in (P.1) of a twin problem. As depicted inFigure 6, on average, 77% of the subjects choose the safe option in (P.1), which is consistent with the revealed aversion toward symmetric risks in simple problems. But this is arguably driven by the fact that, in (P.1) of a twin problem, naive diversification results in choosing the safe option. Again, our findings are robust to splitting the sample in different ways (seeAppendix Efor details). In particular, we observe the same behavior for all the different twin problems that we implement (seeFigures E.7–E.9). This last finding is striking because, for the twin problems parameterized byγ′=0, choosing the safe option in (P.2) does not require any computations. Recall that, in (P.2), assetX2pays its expected value with certainty ifγ′=0. This suggests that complexity in itself can change how people approach a problem; here, it makes them completely ignore the fact that one option obviously allows them to avoid any risk.
article.5. On the Mechanism Underlying the Main: We start by showing the robustness of our main experimental result to other forms of complexity. We then provide additional evidence on response times and memory that support our salience-based explanation of the interaction of skewness and complexity that we observe. 5.1. Robustness to Other Forms of Complexity We lack a theory of what makes a choice problem complex, but it is clear that computational complexity is only part of the picture. We implement portfolio selection problems with larger choice sets—including up to 101 different options—to establish that our main experimental result is robust to other forms of complexity. By increasing the number of available portfolios, we add a form of comparative complexity: on top of having to calculate the portfolio outcomes, subjects have to further evaluate and compare a large number of options.17 Figure 7describes the portfolios chosen in computationally and comparatively complex problems. In these problems, subjects have to decide on the exact shares that they want to invest in the two assets.18We again start by discussing the results for the treatmentSkewed. As illustrated in the left panel ofFigure 7, also when choosing from a large set of available options, a majority of subjects picks a highly right-skewed portfolio by investing a larger share in the right-skewed asset. This observation is consistent with our model.19We conclude that neither computational nor comparative complexity affects the choice among highly skewed portfolios. When the assets of a Mao pair (and the resulting portfolios) are less skewed, however, a large majority invests equal shares in both assets, thereby choosing the safe option. And, as illustrated in the right panel ofFigure 7, this pattern of naive diversification is even more pronounced in (P.1) of the twin problems. Going further, also in (P.2)—in which naive diversification does not yield the safe option—the modal choice is to invest equal shares in both assets. As a consequence, in (P.2), the share of subjects choosing the safe option drops to as little as 14% (compared with 42% in the computationally complex versions and 74% in the simple versions). These findings lend further support to the idea that complexity (in whatever form) affects choices absent a salient cue. In sum, the behavior in computationally and comparatively complex problems supports the main implication of our model: skewness and complexity interact in shaping portfolio selection. 5.2. Strength of Preferences and Response Times The core idea of our model is that complexity can change the way in which subjects approach a problem. In particular, we assume that, absent a salient cue, subjects diversify naively irrespective of the monetary consequences. Because the availability of a salient cue is tied to the skewness of the assets, this results in an interaction of skewness and complexity. Observing an interaction of skewness and complexity does not provide conclusive evidence for our model, however. A natural concern is that this interaction of skewness and complexity is simply the result of skewness preferences being rather strong (in a utility sense). In fact, if the preference for highly right-skewed risks is stronger than the aversion toward symmetric risks and if complexity makes decisions noisier, a classic random utility model (Luce 1958,McFadden 1980) can generate an interaction of skewness and complexity.20Applying the methodology developed inAlós-Ferrer et al. (2021), we can use data on response times to rule out this alternative explanation. The idea is simple. Consistent with evidence from psychology, larger utility differences should translate into fewer random errors and/or faster decisions.21Hence, if the interaction of skewness and complexity is simply driven by skewness preferences being particularly strong, at least one of the following two conditions has to hold in simple problems: either more subjects choose the right-skewed risks than avoid the symmetric ones (i.e., we observe fewer errors with skewed risks) or subjects are at least not systematically slower in choosing right-skewed risks than in avoiding symmetric ones. We find that neither condition holds. First, in simple problems, roughly 74% of the subjects inSymmetricchoose the safe option, whereas only around 70% of the subjects inSkewedchoose a highly right-skewed lottery. Second, as illustrated inFigure 8, subjects choose faster (in the sense of first-order stochastic dominance) inSymmetricthan they do inSkewed. Hence, the interaction of skewness and complexity that we observe does not simply derive from skewness preferences being stronger than the aversion toward symmetric risks. This suggests that skewness and complexity do more than just shifting utility and introducing noise; something more fundamental has to change in how subjects reach a decision in complex problems with and without a salient cue. Our model does not make predictions on how response times vary with complexity. On the one hand, the availability of a salient cue might enable subjects to reach a decision fairly quickly even in objectively complex problems. On the other hand, when facing a complex problem without a salient cue, subjects might be overwhelmed and, thus, give up quickly. Consistent with the first consideration, we find that computational complexity slows down decisions in (P.2) of a twin problem more than it does in problems with highly skewed assets (seeFigure E.12). This suggests that the extreme outcomes of highly skewed assets indeed help with reaching a decision (instead of, say, increasing the engagement with a complex problem). At the same time, when comparing problems with highly skewed assets to (P.1) of a twin problem, we observe the opposite pattern: computational complexity slows down decisions less for symmetric than for skewed assets. We should keep in mind, however, that, in (P.1) of a twin problem, the two assets have identical marginals, which makes naive diversification the obvious choice. Whereas it is hard to interpret the observed response times through the lens of our model, data on a subject’s memory of outcomes will help us to shed more light on the mechanism. 5.3. Contrasts Shape Memory of and, Arguably, Attention Paid to Outcomes Using the memory data elicited at the end of the experiment, we can establish a more direct link between contrast in outcomes and the attention paid to different states. After their last decision, we asked subjects—by surprise—to recall the outcomes of the assets they saw on the preceding screen. Obviously, paying attention to an outcome is necessary for recalling it later (a key feature of the model inGagnon-Bartsch et al. 2023). It also seems sensible to assume that a better recall of an outcome indicates that more attention was paid in the first place (seeHartzmark et al. 2021,Valletti and Veiga 2021for supporting evidence). We, thus, compare recall of more and less salient outcomes to draw inferences on whether salience directs attention. To pin down how the salience of outcomes affects memory thereof, we rely on the notions of relative variance,E[(X1−X2)2], and relative skewness,|E[(X1−X2)3]|/Var(X1−X2)3. The relative variance of the assets measures the size of contrasts in outcomes across the different states of the world. And, as discussed inSection 2, their relative skewness measures the asymmetry of contrasts in outcomes across states and thereby the relative salience of the different states of the world. We regress the percentage share of correctly recalled outcomes on the assets’ relative variance and skewness as well as on several controls using ordinary least squares (OLS) (seeTable 3for the results). Table 3.Determinants of Recall Table 3.Determinants of Recall Notes.Results of OLS regressions with the percentage share of correctly recalled outcomes as the dependent variable and relative variance and skewness of the assets as the main independent variables. We add controls for the number of states, a subject’s skills, and the time a subject spent on the memory task and on the decision task that is to be recalled. Significance level: **5%, ***1%. We find that a more asymmetric contrast in outcomes—as measured by a higher relative skewness—predicts the share of correctly recalled outcomes with a positive sign. In particular, increasing the relative skewness from zero to its highest value in the sample increases the share of correctly recalled outcomes by 12.5 to 13.2 p.p., on average. This is more than the average performance difference for subjects with below- and above-median skills. We further observe that, even when controlling for how much time a subject spent on the portfolio selection problem that the subject has to recall, relative skewness has predictive power. Put differently, asymmetric contrasts in outcomes improve the recall of these outcomes in a way that is not captured by the time spent on the decision screen. This last finding directly speaks to the idea that asymmetric contrasts generate salience, making the corresponding outcomes come to mind more easily.22 The relative variance (i.e., the simple size of contrasts), on the other hand, has no predictive power. The fact that the assets’ relative skewness but not their relative variance is predictive of a subject’s recall is particularly interesting in light of models on deliberate or rational inattention (e.g.,Sims 2003,Gabaix 2014,Caplin and Dean 2015). Models of deliberate inattention typically assume that the attention devoted to a certain dimension of a problem (here, a state of the world) depends on the variance or the size of contrasts in outcomes. We find, however, that it is the asymmetry of such contrasts that shapes memory and arguably also attention. Because of a coding error regarding one of the outcomes, the memory results presented in this section should be interpreted with a little bit of caution, however. We did not force subjects to do the memory task (seeFigure D.9), and all missing answers were coded as zero, which is also the lower outcome of the left-skewed lottery for the more skewed Mao pairs. To address this problem, we count a correct answer of zero if and only if a subject has entered nonzero answers for all the other outcomes, which indicates that the subject made these entries actively.23Whereas we cannot conclusively rule out that we overestimate the number of correctly recalled outcomes for the more skewed Mao pairs, this adjustment might even bias the results against our hypothesis that subjects better recall the outcomes of more skewed assets.
article.6. Related Literature: Our paper builds on two separate strands of the literature, studying the role of complexity and the implications of salience-driven attention for economic behavior. No previous paper, however, systematically studies our main question: is the effect of complexity on choices mitigated by the presence of a salient cue? By studying behavior in portfolio selection problems, we further contribute to the (experimental) finance literature on the drivers of underdiversification. 6.1. Complexity and Economic Choice A growing literature studies the implications of complexity for economic choice.Abeler and Jäger (2015)andBreunig et al. (2021)investigate how people react to complex incentive schemes, and they find that complexity can lead to an adoption of simpler choice rules and, consequently, lower the responsiveness to incentives (see alsoEnke et al. 2023). Similarly,Banovetz and Oprea (2023)show that, if the optimal choice procedure becomes more complex—for example, more demanding in terms of information subjects have to track and memorize—subjects turn to much simpler choice rules. Several studies find that the demand effect of shrouding surcharges is more pronounced for multiplicative rather than additive surcharges (e.g.,Morwitz et al. 1998,Kalaycı and Serra-Garcia 2016). This points to an interaction of computational complexity (which is arguably higher for multiplicative surcharges) and price salience—a hypothesis that alsoKalaycı and Serra-Garcia (2016)state and confirm. We instead systematically study the interaction of salience and complexity in choice under risk. There is no universal definition of choice complexity, however.Oprea (2020)directly measures the complexity of a choice procedure by eliciting a subject’s willingness to pay for not having to implement it again in the future. We, on the contrary, focus on well-defined notions of computational and comparative complexity in arguably uncontroversial cases. Our notion of computational complexity is motivated by the observation that people often struggle with the reduction of compound lotteries (Kahneman and Tversky 1979,Wilcox 1993,Camerer and Ho 1994). To isolate the effect of computational complexity and to abstract from attitudes to (perceived) ambiguity,24we use portfolios with deterministic shares invested in two lotteries instead of compound lotteries. Our notion of comparative complexity builds on the large literature on choice overload (for a meta-study, seeChernev et al. 2015), which documents that an increase in the number of available options can result in subjects switching to simpler choice rules (e.g., choosing easily justifiable options). There are certainly other relevant notions of complexity, such as the number of distinct dimensions that an option has (seeHerman and Bahrick 1966, for an early treatment), on which we do not focus. Instead, using the precisely defined notions of computational and comparative complexity, we document an interaction effect of the complexity of a portfolio selection problem and the availability of a salient cue. 6.2. Salience Effects in Economic Choice The importance of (different types of) salience effects is documented across various domains, including public economics (e.g.,Chetty et al. 2009,Taubinsky and Rees-Jones 2018), finance (e.g.,Frydman and Wang 2020,Cosemans and Frehen 2021,Cakici and Zaremba 2022), industrial organization (e.g.,Bordalo et al. 2016,Dertwinkel-Kalt et al. 2019), and political economy (e.g.,Bordalo et al. 2021,Nunnari and Zápal 2022), as well as basic strategic interactions (e.g.,Crawford and Iriberri 2007,Li and Camerer 2022). We focus on a particular notion of salience that is based on the contrast effect as formalized in salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012).25Our study adds to both the theoretical (Bordalo et al. 2012,Dertwinkel-Kalt and Köster 2020,Lanzani 2022) and experimental (Frydman and Mormann 2018,Dertwinkel-Kalt and Köster 2020) literature that studies contrast effects in choice under risk. In particular, by showing that salience theory cannot account for portfolio selection with symmetric assets, we document its limits in explaining choice under risk. This confirms the conclusions drawn inDertwinkel-Kalt and Köster (2020)andDertwinkel-Kalt and Frey (2024)that salience theory is mainly a model of skewness preferences.26 6.3. Salience-Driven vs. Deliberate (In)attention We postulate that attention and, consequently, behavior is driven by salience. A separate strand of the literature assumes that people optimally deploy attention so that it best serves their goals (e.g.,Sims 2003,Gabaix 2014,Matějka and McKay 2015). Our extension of salience theory shares some similarities with these models. As inGabaix (2014), for instance, we assume that subjects rely on a simple default heuristic in case they have no clue on what to do. Unlike in his model, however, reverting to the default heuristic is not the result of trading off the costs and benefits of being attentive and making a deliberate choice, but it depends on whether there is a salient cue that guides behavior. It is not obvious how to apply classic models of deliberate inattention to our setting.27Still, when extending the intuition underlying these models (i.e., the agent pays attention if and only if there is enough variation in outcomes), one might arrive at a condition similar to (3), in which the left-hand side is replaced by a symmetric measure of the contrast in outcomes (e.g., the relative variance of the assets). Our experimental results suggest, however, that it is the asymmetry of contrasts in outcomes rather than the size of contrasts that shapes attention (and behavior).28 6.4. Evidence on Portfolio Underdiversification Our study also adds to the (experimental) literature on portfolio selection. Existing experiments exclusively look at portfolio selection problems with symmetric assets and document quite robust evidence for naive diversification (e.g.,Kallir and Sonsino 2009,Baltussen and Post 2011,Ackert et al. 2015,Eyster and Weizsäcker 2016,Ungeheuer and Weber 2021). We qualify these findings by showing that subjects in our experiment do not diversify naively if the assets are sufficiently skewed. At the same time, we contribute to the debate on how to model preferences over skewed portfolios. As we argue inAppendix C, our experimental design allows us to directly test models on a variance–skewness trade-off (e.g.,Mitton and Vorkink 2007), which assume that people seek positive skewness but dislike variance per se. In contrast to this theory, our subjects seek variance in right-skewed portfolios, which yields additional support for a salience-based explanation of skewness preferences. Finally, we add to the literature that associates portfolio underdiversification with a lack of financial literacy (e.g.,Lusardi and Mitchell 2011,2014). Whereas, in our experiment, more literate subjects are less likely to diversify naively, we find no effect of literacy on skewness-driven underdiversification (seeFigure E.15). This suggests that financial training may not reduce underdiversification in many instances, namely, when a salient cue is present.
article.7. Concluding Remarks: We document that the presence of a salient cue mitigates the effect of complexity on choices, which, in turn, can result in (more) consistent behavior across differently complex problems. Whereas we take a purely positive perspective in this paper, our experimental results do raise important normative questions. In economics, “Rationality is [often] equated with consistency” (Halevy and Mayraz 2024, p. 2). On the contrary, the model we develop to rationalize our findings does not equate consistent behavior with normatively appealing behavior. Salient cues might, for example, draw a consumer’s attention toward irrelevant aspects of the available options. Or, asKőszegi and Szeidl (2013, p. 80) put it, “Consumers make better decisions in more balanced choices” without eye-catching features. Our experimental results further stress the point that it might be problematic to equate consistency with rationality. At the same time, it is not obvious thatKőszegi and Szeidl’s (2013)(implicit) recommendation to remove whatever makes a choice unbalanced necessarily improves a consumer’s decisions. Our findings instead suggest that, in complex problems without a salient cue, people might give up completely and revert to even simpler rules of thumb such as naive diversification. Whether this improves decisions in complex problems likely depends on the context. This highlights the need for future research to investigate the welfare implications of changing behavior by providing salient cues. article.Appendix B. Predictions of Salience Theo: B.1. An Application of Salience Theory to Portfolio Selection Problems B.1.1. Model. We start by adapting salience theory (Bordalo et al. 2012) to a general portfolio selection problem(A,C). As before, we denote asFthe joint cumulative distribution function (CDF) ofX1andX2. A salient thinker can deal with the computational complexity of portfolio selection problems; that is, the salient thinker evaluates a portfolio based on the distribution of outcomes it implements. Comparative complexity, on the other hand, can affect the behavior of a salient thinker by changing the reference portfolio and thereby the salience of outcomes.29 B.2. As long as the level effect is not too strong relative to the contrast effect, which is guaranteed by homogeneity of degree zero, this sharp increase in the asymmetry of contrasts in outcomes is sufficient to induce a salient thinker to take some risk. Mean–Variance–Skewness Preferences With our results on comparatively complex portfolio selection problems, we can contribute to the broader debate on how to model skewness preferences. The typical assumption in the (empirical) finance literature is that people trade off variance for skewness.Mitton and Vorkink (2007), for example, extend the mean–variance framework to also incorporate a preference for skewness. Their model predicts that subjects minimize variance for a given level of skewness. Behavioral models of choice under risk, on the other hand, predict that the attitude toward variance varies with skewness (seeAppendix BorBarberis and Huang 2008,Bordalo et al. 2013a). Using the perfectly negatively correlated Mao pairs, we can precisely test both approaches. By part I ofLemma 1, for a perfectly negatively correlated Mao pair, the variance of the resulting portfolios depends on the exact shareαinvested in the right-skewed asset, whereas their skewness only depends on whetherαis smaller or larger than12. If subjects indeed minimize the variance in portfolio returns for a given level of skewness, we should observe that, conditional on choosing a right-skewed portfolio, they invest just slightly more than 50% in the right-skewed asset. Behavioral models such as salience theory, on the other hand, predict that subjects invest significantly more than 50% in the right-skewed asset if the Mao pair is sufficiently skewed. This follows from the fact that, according to these models, positive skewness renders (some) variance attractive. Mao pairs, thus, allow for a clean test of these two competing approaches. As illustrated inFigure C.1, subjects do not minimize variance for a given level of skewness but instead seek (some) variance when choosing a positively skewed portfolio. Strikingly, for the more skewed Mao pairs, subjects who choose a right-skewed portfolio almost maximize the variance in portfolio returns. Even for less skewed Mao pairs, subjects choose portfolios with a substantial level of variance. The results are consistent with salience theory and other behavioral models (such as CPT) but inconsistent with a variance–skewness trade-off. C.4. The Visual Salience Model C.4.1. Model. Questions of a Modified Cognitive Reflection Test. The following questions represent slight modifications of the cognitive reflection tests as used byFrederick (2005)andPrimi et al. (2016). If 10 machines take 10 minutes to make 10 nails, how many minutes do 100 machines need to make 100 nails? A part of a pond is covered with water lilies. article.Appendix E. Additional Experimental Resu: E.1. Robustness of the Main Experimental Results inSkewed In this section, we present additional results on the robustness of our main experimental findings in the treatmentSkewed(seeFigure 5). As discussed in the main text, we find similar results when controlling for the variance in portfolio returns (seeFigures E.1andE.2) as well as when splitting the sample (at the median) by cognitive skills (seeFigures E.3andE.4). E.2. Robustness of the Main Experimental Results inSymmetric In this section, we present additional results on the robustness of our main experimental findings in the treatmentSymmetric(seeFigure 6). As discussed in the main text, we find similar results when splitting the sample by cognitive skills (seeFigures E.5andE.6). Moreover, we observe similar behavior across the three different twin problems (seeFigures E.7–E.9). E.3. Experimental Results on Positively Correlated Mao Pairs Figure E.10illustrates the results for positively correlated Mao lotteries. Again, subjects mostly avoid left-skewed risks but seek sufficiently skewed right-skewed ones. And, just as with negatively correlated Mao lotteries, we find similar patterns in simple and computationally complex problems. These findings are consistent with salience theory (and our extension thereof). But the findings inFigure E.10are inconsistent with specific functional forms that have been used in the literature (e.g.,Bordalo et al. 2013b). ByProposition B.5, if the salience function is homogeneous of degree zero, subjects should seek only left-skewed risks for the less skewed Mao pairs. We find, however, that around 50% of the subjects choose mildly right-skewed risks in simple problems. This behavior cannot be rationalized with a salience function that satisfies homogeneity of degree zero. Similarly, we observe that a majority of subjects diversifies naively in comparatively complex problems with mildly skewed assets (seeFigure E.11). Under homogeneity of degree zero, however, we would expect a salient thinker to choose a left-skewed portfolio in this case (seeProposition B.6). In sum, whereas consistent with our model, the results on positively correlated Mao pairs impose restrictions on the form of the salience function. E.4. Additional Results on Response Times In this section, we study the effect of (computational) complexity on the time it takes subjects to reach a decision. InFigure E.12, we plot the empirical cumulative densities of the differences in response times in computationally complex and simple problems. We find that the distribution in (P.2) of a twin problem first order stochastically dominates that for problems with highly skewed assets (see the bold lines inFigure E.12). Hence, computational complexity slows down decisions in (P.2) of a twin problem more than it does in problems with highly skewed assets. As argued in the text, this is consistent with the idea that salient cues help subjects to reach a decision. When comparing problems with highly skewed assets to (P.1) of a twin problem, we observe the opposite pattern: computational complexity slows down decisions less for symmetric than for skewed assets. This is likely driven by the fact that, in (P.1), naive diversification is the obvious choice so that computational complexity does not make the problem much harder. E.5. Additional Memory Data In this section, we focus on complex portfolio selection problems based on perfectly negatively correlated Mao lotteries (inSkewed) or the symmetric limit thereof (inSymmetric). These problems are most comparable (seeSection 3for details) and, thus, allow for the cleanest test of how salience shapes memory. We find that subjects are better in recalling the outcomes of highly skewed lotteries than in recalling the outcomes of less skewed or even symmetric lotteries (seeFigure E.13). The distribution of correctly recalled outcomes for the more skewed Mao pairs first order stochastically dominates the distributions for the less skewed or symmetric assets. Because, in (P.1) of a twin problem, there are fewer distinct outcomes to be remembered (namely, two instead of four), the cleanest comparison that we can make is the one between the differently skewed Mao pairs.32As illustrated inFigure E.14, subjects are more likely to correctly recall the outcomes of the more skewed Mao pairs, in particular, the more salient outcomes in the state with the larger (absolute) contrast in outcomes,Δ(V,S)=L(E,V,−S)−L(E,V,S). This observation is consistent with the idea of salience-driven attention, and it can explain why, for Mao pairs withS= 2.7, the modal number of correctly recalled outcomes is two out of four. Figure E.14.Recall of Individual Outcomes Figure E.14.Recall of Individual Outcomes More specifically, with the caveat of the coding problem in mind, we observe that, for Mao pairs withS= 2.7, a large majority of subjects correctly recalls the zero outcome of the left-skewed asset. This might be unsurprising as the outcome of zero is not only salient in terms of our model, but also the only single-digit number on the screen. Reassuringly, a substantial share of subjects correctly recalls also the nonzero outcome in this state, namely, the right-skewed asset’s large upside. We find similar patterns for the case of the maximal positive correlation. E.6. Cognitive Skills Drive Naive Diversification, Not Skewness Preferences Many studies (e.g.,Graham et al. 2009) find that sophistication improves financial performance (for surveys of the literature seeHastings et al. 2013,Lusardi and Mitchell 2014). In our experiment, however, low financial literacy and low cognitive skills are associated with underdiversification only in the case of symmetric assets but not in the case of skewed assets. As depicted in the left panel ofFigure E.15, in the treatmentSkewed, there is zero correlation between a subject’s cognitive skills—as measured by the share of correctly answered financial literacy, modified CRT, and math questions—and the share of safe options chosen (over the course of the experiment) by this subject. In the treatmentSymmetric, however, we do observe a positive correlation between our measure of cognitive skills and the share of safe options chosen during the experiment (see the right panel ofFigure E.15).33In sum, we conclude that naive diversification but not skewness-related underdiversification is sensitive to cognitive skills.

Statement of the Result We are interested in how robust the qualitative predictions of salience theory—as derived inSection 3.1—are to increasing the number of available options. We adopt the salience model introduced inAppendix Bwith a reference point given by the state-wise average over all alternative options, and we assume throughout this section that the salience function is homogeneous of degree zero. Suppose the choice set isC={L(E1,V1,S1),…,L(En,Vn,Sn)}for somen∈N≥3. To obtain clean and simple results, we again assume that all options are binary lotteries with the same expected value,Ei=Efor someE∈R>0, and that one option pays this expected value with certainty. Moreover, all lotteries (except for the safe option) have the same absolute skewness:Si∈{−S,S}for someS∈R. Finally, to keep the state space as small as possible, let all lotteries (except for the safe option) be perfectly correlated with each other.34 Even when enlarging the choice set and making the problem comparatively more complex, salience theory predicts a preference for sufficiently right-skewed risks and an aversion toward sufficiently left-skewed risks. Skewness preferences are, thus, robust to comparative complexity. (Robust Skewness Preferences).For anyn∈N≥3, there exist threshold valuesS′andS″withS′<0<S″such that the following two statements hold: Ifmax{S1,…,Sn}>S″, then a salient thinker chooses one of the right-skewed lotteries. Ifmax{S1,…,Sn}<S′, then a salient thinker chooses the safe option E. Our second result is illustrated inFigure F.1: when enlarging the choice set, a salient thinker might choose a symmetric binary lottery over its expected value. Hence, the aversion toward symmetric risks is less robust (to comparative complexity) than skewness preferences. When compared with its expected value, the contrast on the upside and downside of a symmetric binary lottery is identical. This implies that already a relatively small change to the reference point—through adding a suitably chosen third option to the choice set—renders the upside of a symmetric lottery salient. For very skewed lotteries, on the other hand, contrasts on the upside and downside are highly asymmetric to start with so that the reference point would have to change by a lot for salience theory to no longer predict skewness preferences. The robustness of skewness preferences described inProposition F.1relies—to some extent at least—on the particular lotteries we consider. By adding other options to the choice set, we could also create a situation in which a salient thinker avoids even highly right-skewed risks or chooses highly left-skewed ones.35But, because of the extreme outcomes of sufficiently skewed risks, the reference point would have to move a lot in the right direction for salience theory to no longer predict skewness preferences. In this sense, the statement that skewness preferences are more robust than the aversion toward symmetric risks is valid also in more general settings. F.2.
